figures.caption: Figure 1: Top: the firm example described in the introduction with no contracts and no prosociality. Bottom: contracts can prevent defection, but rational agents select the second-best outcome (red). Prosocial agents select the optimal outcome (green).
Figure 5: Joint action selection frequencies for Prisoner’s Dilemma.

Figure 8: We show one specific example of five trajectories with GPT-4o, prosocial agents, under Code Contract in the stochastic setting. For clarity of presentation, each month is split in two phases, the first phase is regeneration and the second phase is cumulative harvesting amount.
Figure 7: Total gain and survival months by model, contract regime, and prosociality level.

Figure 3: The stages of the activities in GovSimContract during each month. We introduce different discussion frameworks into GovSim to allow for creation of the contracts. Agents keep their memories when harvesting. In the contract case, they also see the contract in force. The Table Games share stage 2 with GovSimContract, with differences explained in Section 4.2.

Table 1: Utilitarian efficiency, under incontractibility, of different models and regimes for the Prisoner’s Dilemma and Stag Hunt. We observe improvement with respect to the Selfish, no contract baseline from contract regimes and prosociality.
Figure 2: Aggregated GovSimContract results across models. Prosociality provides, on average, better outcomes than contracts.
Figure 6: Joint action selection frequencies for Stag Hunt.
Figure 4: Frequency of contract primitives in generated Python contracts
